body_txt: Not just any big bad wolf. The Big Bad Wolf. This is an archetypal character who appears as an antagonist in multiple Fairy Tales and Fables , most famously The Three Little Pigs and Little Red Riding Hood . In both stories, the main antagonist is a wolf who can talk , and he tries to eat the protagonist/s. To be clear, there isn't literally a single individual Big Bad Wolf shared between these stories, they just both have a hungry Savage Wolf as their main threat. There are also other less famous stories with similar wolf characters like The Wolf and the Seven Young Kids , Peter and the Wolf , and various ones from Aesop's Fables , the most famous being The Boy Who Cried Wolf . He oftenly tries to prey on pigs, sheep, children , Little Dead Riding Hood , or Woodland Critters . He might have a knack for disguising himself (used to fool Little Red Riding Hood) and/or powerful lungs (used to destroy two of the Three Little Pigs' houses). The name "Big Bad Wolf" was popularized by Disney 's adaptation of The Three Little Pigs . They treated their adaptations of Little Red Riding Hood and The Boy Who Cried Wolf as sequels to it (making the Big Bad Wolf a Composite Character ), which basically cemented the name of the trope. It should be mentioned that he isn't always a literal wolf in modern versions; since the seventies or so he's often a werewolf . It's also common for modern versions, specially those that are more child/family-oriented, to have the Big Bad Wolf treated as more of a Public Domain Character and an individual who doesn't necessarily have to fill the traditional role he had in his origin stories. Since the traits that get used depend on which stories are being adapted and Alternative Character Interpretation happens all the time, do point out how the Big Bad Wolf is portrayed when adding examples. Not to Be Confused with the trope Big Bad . For big wolves (whether or not, strictly speaking, bad), please see Canis Major . For the movie named after the character, see Big Bad Wolf .
